51→ 52→### Lifecycle Overview 53→ 54→``` 55→┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ 56→│ Create │────▶│ Submit │────▶│ Dispatch │────▶│ Implement │────▶│ Merge │ 57→│ Plan │ │ Plan │ │ Workflow │ │ Plan │ │ PR │ 58→└─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ 59→ │ │ │ │ │ 60→ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ 61→ GitHub Issue git branch GitHub Actions Code Changes Issue Closed 62→ with erk-plan creates branch finds existing committed via commit 63→ label + draft PR PR and executes and pushed message 64→``` 65→ 66→### Key File Locations at a Glance 67→ 68→| Location | Purpose | 69→| ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | 70→| `~/.claude/plans/*.md` | Local plan storage (sorted by modification time) | 71→| `.impl/plan.md` | Immutable plan in worktree (local implementation) | 72→| `.impl/progress.md` | Mutable progress tracking | 73→| `.impl/plan-ref.json` | Plan reference (provider-agnostic, replaces legacy issue.json) | 74→| `.impl/run-info.json` | GitHub Actions run reference (remote only) | 75→| `.erk/impl-context/` | Remote implementation folder (GitHub Actions) | 76→ 77→### Which Phase Am I In? 78→ 79→| Observable State | Current Phase | `lifecycle_stage` | 80→| --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ----------------- | 81→| Issue has `erk-plan` label, no comments | Phase 1: Created | `planned` | 82→| Issue has `submission-queued` comment | Phase 2: Submitted | `planned` | 83→| Issue has `workflow-started` comment | Phase 3: Dispatched | `impl` | 84→| PR is draft, workflow running | Phase 4: Implementing | `impl` | 85→| PR is ready for review | Phase 5: Complete | `impl` | 86→| Issue is CLOSED | Merged (PR closed the issue) | — | 87→ 88→**Note:** The `lifecycle_stage` field in plan-header metadata provides a machine-readable equivalent of these observable states. See [Lifecycle Stage Tracking](#lifecycle-stage-tracking) for details. 89→ 90→### Plan Relevance Assessment 91→ 92→When evaluating whether a plan should be implemented or closed, use the verdict classification system from `/local:check-relevance`: 93→ 94→| Verdict | Overlap | Meaning | 95→| --------------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------- | 96→| SUPERSEDED | >80% | Work is already implemented in master | 97→| PARTIALLY_IMPLEMENTED | 30-80% | Some work exists, plan may need scoping adjustment | 98→| DIFFERENT_APPROACH | N/A | Same problem solved with different implementation | 99→| STILL_RELEVANT | <30% | Work is not yet implemented, plan remains valid | 100→| NEEDS_REVIEW | Unclear | Manual review required, evidence inconclusive | 101→ 102→**Usage:** Run `/local:check-relevance ` to assess a plan's current relevance before deciding to implement or close it. 103→ 104→### Session Idempotency 105→ 106→Plan save operations are idempotent within a session. The `plan-save` command: 107→ 108→1. Checks if a plan issue was already created for this session ID 109→2. If found, returns the existing issue instead of creating a duplicate 110→3. Uses `_get_existing_saved_issue()` helper to query GitHub 111→ 112→This prevents duplicate issues when retry loops occur (e.g., hook blocking → retry → would-be duplicate). 113→ 114→### Plan Storage Lookup Priority 115→ 116→When looking up plan files, the system checks in order: 117→ 118→| Priority | Location | Condition | 119→| -------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------- | 120→| 1 | `--plan-file` argument | Always checked first | 121→| 2 | `.erk/scratch/sessions/{id}/` | With `--session-id` | 122→| 3 | `~/.claude/plans/` (by mtime) | Fallback | 123→ 124→See [Plan Lookup Strategy](plan-lookup-strategy.md) for details on session-scoped lookups. 125→ 126→--- 127→ 128→## Understanding Investigation Findings 129→ 130→When a plan includes an "Investigation Findings" section, these are **mandatory corrections** that MUST be incorporated, not optional suggestions. 131→ 132→### What Investigation Findings Are 133→ 134→Investigation findings appear in plans (especially erk-learn consolidation plans) after an agent: 135→ 136→1. **Validates original assumptions** - Checks if files exist, features are implemented, APIs work as documented 137→2. **Discovers reality mismatches** - Finds when original plans referenced non-existent files, outdated APIs, or completed work 138→3. **Documents corrections** - Records what actually exists vs what was planned 139→ 140→**Example from a real plan:** 141→ 142→```markdown 143→## Investigation Findings 144→ 145→### Corrections to Original Plans 146→ 147→- **#6134**: `prompt-executor-gateway.md` still references Haiku - confirmed needs update 148→- **#6131**: `preprocessing.md` exists (81 lines) - needs UPDATE not CREATE 149→- **#6130**: Pattern templates don't exist anywhere - confirmed CREATE needed 150→``` 151→ 152→### Why They Matter 153→ 154→Without investigation findings, agents would: 155→ 156→- Create duplicate files that already exist 157→- Update non-existent files 158→- Implement features that are already complete 159→- Reference APIs that have changed 160→ 161→Investigation findings **correct the plan** to match current reality. 162→ 163→### How to Use Investigation Findings 164→ 165→When implementing a plan with investigation findings: 166→ 167→1. **Read them first** - Before starting implementation, understand what changed 168→2. **Trust them completely** - They reflect actual codebase investigation 169→3. **Follow their directives** - "UPDATE not CREATE" means use Edit, not Write 170→4. **Don't second-guess** - The investigation was thorough 171→ 172→**Anti-pattern:** Skipping investigation findings and following original plan items that were later corrected. 173→ 174→**Correct pattern:** Use investigation findings to understand what actions to take, then refer to implementation steps for execution order. 175→ 176→--- 177→ 178→## Phase 1: Plan Creation 179→ 180→Plans can be created through two paths: interactive (via Claude) or CLI (direct). 181→ 182→### Interactive Path: Plan Mode + `/erk:plan-save` 183→ 184→The interactive path uses Claude's plan mode for guided plan creation: 185→ 186→```bash 187→# 1. Enter Plan Mode (automatic for complex tasks) 188→# 2. Create plan interactively 189→# 3. Exit Plan Mode 190→# 4. Save to GitHub: 191→/erk:plan-save 192→``` 193→ 194→This workflow: 195→ 196→1. Claude enters Plan Mode for the task 197→2. Plan creation with context extraction 198→3. Plan saved to `~/.claude/plans/*.md` on Exit Plan Mode 199→4. `/erk:plan-save` creates GitHub Issue with `erk-plan` label 200→ 201→### CLI Path: `erk pr create --file ` 202→ 203→Direct plan creation from a file: 204→ 205→```bash 206→erk pr create --file my-plan.md 207→``` 208→ 209→This creates a GitHub Issue directly from the plan file. 210→ 211→### Plan Storage 212→ 213→Plans are stored in GitHub Issues: 214→ 215→- **Issue body**: Contains `plan-header` metadata block 216→- **First comment**: Contains `plan-body` with full plan content in collapsible details 217→ 218→**Issue body structure:** 219→ 220→````markdown 221→# Plan: [Title] 222→ 223→ 224→ 225→
226→plan-header 227→ 228→```yaml 229→created_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z 230→created_by: username 231→last_dispatched_at: null 232→last_dispatched_run_id: null 233→last_local_impl_at: null 234→lifecycle_stage: planned 235→``` 236→```` 237→ 238→
239→ 240→``` 241→ 242→**First comment structure:** 243→ 244→```markdown 245→ 246→
247→plan-body 248→ 249→[Full plan content here] 250→ 251→
252→ 253→``` 254→ 255→### The `erk-plan` Label 256→ 257→The `erk-plan` label marks issues as implementation plans: 258→ 259→- **Auto-created** if it doesn't exist (green, #0E8A16) 260→- **Required** for submission and implementation 261→- **Validated** before workflow dispatch 262→ 263→--- 264→ 265→## Phase 2: Plan Submission 266→ 267→Submission prepares the plan for remote execution via `erk pr submit `. 268→ 269→**Key responsibility**: `erk pr submit` is the **source of truth** for branch and PR creation. The workflow dispatch (Phase 3) expects these to already exist. 270→ 271→### Pre-Submission Validation 272→ 273→Before submission, the command validates: 274→ 275→1. **Label check**: Issue must have `erk-plan` label 276→2. **State check**: Issue must be OPEN (not closed) 277→3. **Clean working directory**: No uncommitted changes 278→ 279→### Branch Reuse Detection 280→ 281→Before creating a new branch, `erk pr submit` checks for existing local branches matching the plan's branch pattern: 282→ 283→``` 284→Found existing local branch(es) for this issue: 285→ • plnd/feature-01-10-0900 286→ • plnd/feature-01-12-1430 287→ 288→New branch would be: plnd/feature-01-15-1600 289→ 290→Use existing branch 'plnd/feature-01-12-1430'? [Y/n] 291→``` 292→ 293→**Note:** Legacy issue-based plans may still use the `P{issue_number}-*` pattern. Current plans use the `plnd/` prefix. 294→ 295→**User options:** 296→ 297→1. **Use existing** (default): Continue with the most recent branch 298→2. **Delete and create new**: Remove existing branches, start fresh 299→3. **Abort**: Cancel submission 300→ 301→This prevents branch proliferation when resubmitting plans. 302→ 303→### Branch Creation 304→ 305→Branches are created directly via git: 306→ 307→```bash 308→git branch 309→``` 310→ 311→**Branch naming**: Erk computes the branch name using `sanitize_worktree_name()` with a timestamp suffix. Branch names follow the pattern `plnd/{slug}-{timestamp}` where the slug is derived from the plan title. 312→ 313→**Example**: Plan "Add user authentication" → `plnd/add-user-authentic-11-30-1430` 314→ 315→**Legacy format**: Older plans may use the `P{issue}-{slug}-{timestamp}` format (e.g., `P123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430`). The `P{issue}-` prefix is considered legacy; `plan-ref.json` is now the sole source of truth for plan-to-branch mapping. 316→ 317→### Learn Plan Base Branch Selection 318→ 319→Learn plans (issues with `erk-learn` label) use special base branch logic: 320→ 321→1. **Extract parent reference**: Read `learned_from_issue` from plan-header metadata 322→2. **Fetch parent plan**: Get the parent implementation plan's issue 323→3. **Get parent branch**: Extract `branch_name` from parent's plan-header 324→4. **Stack on parent**: Use parent's branch as base instead of trunk 325→ 326→This creates a branch hierarchy: 327→ 328→``` 329→trunk (main) 330→ └── plnd/feature-branch-01-15-1430 (parent implementation) 331→ └── plnd/docs-for-feature-01-16-0900 (learn plan) 332→``` 333→ 334→**Note:** Legacy plans may still use the `P{issue}-` prefix pattern. Current plans use `plnd/` prefix. 335→ 336→**Fallback**: If parent lookup fails (missing parent, no branch recorded), falls back to trunk. 337→ 338→**Implementation**: See `get_learn_plan_parent_branch()` in `src/erk/cli/commands/pr/dispatch_cmd.py`. 339→ 340→### `.erk/impl-context/` Folder Creation 341→ 342→The submit command creates the `.erk/impl-context/` folder structure: 343→ 344→``` 345→.erk/impl-context/ 346→├── plan.md # Full plan content from issue 347→└── ref.json # Plan reference metadata (provider, plan_id, url, etc.) 348→``` 349→ 350→**`ref.json` structure:** 351→ 352→```json 353→{ 354→ "provider": "github", 355→ "plan_id": "123", 356→ "url": "https://github.com/owner/repo/issues/123", 357→ "created_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z", 358→ "synced_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z", 359→ "labels": [], 360→ "objective_id": null 361→} 362→``` 363→ 364→### Draft PR Creation 365→ 366→A draft PR is created locally (for correct commit attribution): 367→ 368→- **Title**: Issue title with "Plan: " prefix stripped 369→- **Body**: Includes checkout instructions and metadata 370→- **State**: Draft (marked ready after implementation) 371→ 372→**Note**: The PR body includes `**Plan:** #` to link back to the issue. Issue closing is handled via commit message keywords ("Closes #N") when the PR is merged. 373→ 374→### `distinct_id` Generation 375→ 376→A 6-character base36 identifier is generated for workflow run discovery: 377→ 378→- Used in workflow `run-name` for matching 379→- Enables polling to find the specific run 380→- Format: `{issue_number}:{distinct_id}` in run display title 381→ 382→### Metadata Update 383→ 384→After submission, the issue receives a `submission-queued` comment with metadata: 385→ 386→```yaml 387→schema: submission-queued 388→queued_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z 389→submitted_by: username 390→issue_number: 123 391→validation_results: 392→ issue_is_open: true 393→ has_erk_plan_label: true 394→expected_workflow: erk-impl 395→``` 396→ 397→--- 398→ 399→## Phase 3: Workflow Dispatch 400→ 401→The `plan-implement.yml` workflow handles remote implementation. 402→ 403→### Workflow Inputs 404→ 405→| Input | Description | 406→| -------------- | ------------------------------------ | 407→| `issue_number` | GitHub issue number to implement | 408→| `submitted_by` | GitHub username of submitter | 409→| `distinct_id` | 6-char base36 for run discovery | 410→| `issue_title` | Issue title for workflow run display | 411→ 412→### Concurrency Control 413→ 414→```yaml 415→concurrency: 416→ group: implement-issue-${{ github.event.inputs.issue_number }} 417→ cancel-in-progress: true 418→``` 419→ 420→This ensures only one implementation runs per issue at a time. 421→ 422→### Workflow Phases 423→ 424→#### Phase 1: Checkout & Setup 425→ 426→- Checkout repository with full history 427→- Install tools: `uv`, `erk`, `claude`, `prettier` 428→- Configure git with submitter identity 429→- Detect trunk branch (main or master) 430→ 431→#### Phase 2: Find PR & Checkout Branch 432→ 433→- Find existing PR via `gh pr list --head ` (by branch, not body search) 434→- Checkout the implementation branch 435→- Update `.erk/impl-context/` with fresh plan content (for reruns) 436→ 437→#### Phase 3: Use Existing PR 438→ 439→- Use existing PR (created by `erk pr submit`) 440→- Post `workflow-started` comment to issue 441→- Update issue body with `last_dispatched_run_id` 442→ 443→#### Phase 4: Implementation 444→ 445→- Copy `.erk/impl-context/` to `.impl/` (Claude reads `.impl/`) 446→- Create `.impl/run-info.json` with workflow run details 447→- Execute `/erk:plan-implement` with Claude 448→ 449→#### Phase 5: Submission 450→ 451→- Stage implementation changes (NOT `.erk/impl-context/` deletion) 452→- Run `/erk:git-pr-push` to create proper commit message 453→- Clean up `.erk/impl-context/` in separate commit 454→- Mark PR ready for review 455→- Update PR body with implementation summary 456→- Trigger CI via empty commit 457→ 458→--- 459→ 460→## Phase 4: Implementation 461→ 462→Implementation executes the plan, whether locally or via GitHub Actions. 463→ 464→### `.erk/impl-context/` vs `.impl/` 465→ 466→| Folder | Purpose | Git Status | 467→| -------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | 468→| `.erk/impl-context/` | Remote implementation (GitHub Actions) | Committed, then deleted | 469→| `.impl/` | Local implementation + Claude's working copy | In `.gitignore`, never committed | 470→ 471→In GitHub Actions, `.erk/impl-context/` is copied to `.impl/` before Claude runs. 472→ 473→### `.impl/run-info.json` 474→ 475→Created in GitHub Actions to track the workflow run: 476→ 477→```json 478→{ 479→ "run_id": "1234567890", 480→ "run_url": "https://github.com/owner/repo/actions/runs/1234567890" 481→} 482→``` 483→ 484→### `/erk:plan-implement` Command 485→ 486→The implementation command: 487→ 488→1. Validates `.impl/` exists with `plan.md` and `progress.md` 489→2. Creates TodoWrite entries for tracking 490→3. Posts start comment to GitHub issue (if linked) 491→4. Executes each phase sequentially 492→5. Updates `progress.md` as steps complete 493→6. Runs CI validation 494→7. Cleans up artifacts 495→ 496→### Progress Tracking 497→ 498→Progress is tracked in `.impl/progress.md`: 499→ 500→```markdown 501→--- 502→completed_nodes: 3 503→total_nodes: 5 504→steps: 505→ - text: "1. First step" 506→ completed: true 507→ - text: "2. Second step" 508→ completed: true 509→ - text: "3. Third step" 510→ completed: true 511→ - text: "4. Fourth step" 512→ completed: false 513→ - text: "5. Fifth step" 514→ completed: false 515→--- 516→ 517→# Progress Tracking 518→ 519→- [x] 1. First step 520→- [x] 2. Second step 521→- [x] 3. Third step 522→- [ ] 4. Fourth step 523→- [ ] 5. Fifth step 524→``` 525→ 526→Progress tracking is done via the TodoWrite tool in the Claude Code session. 527→ 528→### Detecting Queued vs Implemented Plans 529→ 530→A PR associated with a plan may exist but not contain the actual implementation: 531→ 532→- **Queued Plan**: PR contains only `.erk/impl-context/` folder with plan files 533→- **Implemented Plan**: PR contains actual source code changes 534→ 535→To verify implementation status: 536→ 537→1. Check if PR diff includes changes outside `.erk/impl-context/` 538→2. Use `gh pr diff ` and look for actual implementation files 539→3. Don't rely solely on PR state (OPEN/MERGED) - a PR can be open with only plan files 540→ 541→This pattern was discovered when verifying prerequisite PR #5577: the PR existed and was open, but only contained `.erk/impl-context/` plan files, not the actual PlanSynthesizer agent. 542→ 543→### No-Changes Error Scenario 544→ 545→When implementation produces no code changes (duplicate plan, work already merged), the workflow handles this gracefully: 546→ 547→1. **Detects no changes**: Branch has no commits beyond base 548→2. **Creates diagnostic PR**: Updates PR body with diagnostic information 549→3. **Applies `no-changes` label**: Marks PR for user review 550→4. **Posts issue comment**: Links issue to diagnostic PR 551→5. **Exits gracefully**: Returns exit code 0 (success) 552→ 553→**Exit code semantics:** 554→ 555→- Exit 0 = Success (PR updated and ready for review) 556→- Exit 1 = Error (GitHub API failure) 557→ 558→The workflow treats no-changes as successful completion, not an error. Users review the diagnostic PR to determine if work is already done. 559→ 560→See [No Code Changes Handling](no-changes-handling.md) for details. 561→ 562→--- 563→ 564→## Phase 5: PR Finalization & Merge 565→ 566→The final phase prepares the PR for review and merge. 567→ 568→### `/erk:git-pr-push` Submission 569→ 570→The pure git submission flow: 571→ 572→1. Analyze staged changes 573→2. Generate AI commit message 574→3. Commit with proper attribution 575→4. Push to remote 576→5. Update PR body with summary 577→ 578→### `.erk/impl-context/` Cleanup 579→ 580→In GitHub Actions, `.erk/impl-context/` is removed in a separate commit after CI passes: 581→ 582→```bash 583→git rm -rf .erk/impl-context/ 584→git commit -m "Remove .erk/impl-context/ folder after implementation" 585→git push 586→``` 587→ 588→This keeps the implementation commit clean. 589→ 590→#### Timing and Distinction 591→ 592→The cleanup happens in a specific sequence: 593→ 594→1. **Implementation commit** - Contains the actual code changes 595→2. **CI validation** - Tests, formatting, type checking must pass 596→3. **Remove `.erk/impl-context/`** - Cleanup commit (this step) 597→4. **Push changes** - Both commits pushed to PR 598→ 599→**Key distinction:** 600→ 601→- **`.erk/impl-context/`**: CI automatically removes this after validation passes. This is the ephemeral working copy used during remote implementation. It's safe to delete because it's already been copied to `.impl/` for Claude's use. 602→ 603→- **`.impl/`**: Requires user review and manual deletion. This is the original plan that should be preserved until the user confirms completion. Never auto-delete this folder - it serves as documentation of what was planned vs. what was implemented. 604→ 605→**Clear sequence**: CI passes → remove `.erk/impl-context/` → commit → push 606→ 607→This pattern ensures that transient artifacts (`.erk/impl-context/`) are cleaned up automatically while permanent artifacts (`.impl/`) remain for user review. 608→ 609→### PR Ready for Review 610→ 611→```bash 612→gh pr ready "$BRANCH_NAME" 613→``` 614→ 615→Marks the draft PR as ready for review. 616→ 617→### PR Body Update 618→ 619→The PR body is updated with: 620→ 621→1. Implementation summary (from commit message) 622→2. Standardized footer from `get-pr-body-footer` 623→3. Checkout instructions 624→ 625→### CI Trigger 626→ 627→An empty commit triggers push-event workflows: 628→ 629→```bash 630→git commit --allow-empty -m "Trigger CI workflows" 631→git push 632→``` 633→ 634→This is needed because workflow dispatch doesn't trigger PR workflows. 635→ 636→### Auto-Close on Merge 637→ 638→GitHub automatically closes the linked issue when the PR is merged if the commit message contains "Closes #N" or similar keywords. 639→ 640→The `gt finalize` command (used during PR finalization) adds the closing keyword to the commit message, ensuring the issue is closed when the PR merges. 641→ 642→--- 643→ 644→## Objective Roadmap Integration 645→ 646→When implementing a plan that corresponds to an objective roadmap step, the workflow includes automatic roadmap updates via markers. 647→ 648→### Marker-Based State Management 649→ 650→1. **Create markers during planning:** 651→ - `objective-context` marker: stores objective issue number (read by plan-save to link the plan to its parent objective) 652→ - `roadmap-step` marker: stores step ID (e.g., "1C.2") for automatic roadmap updates 653→ 654→2. **Automatic roadmap update on plan save:** 655→ - `/erk:plan-save` checks for `roadmap-step` marker 656→ - If present, runs `erk exec update-objective-node` to update the objective's roadmap table with the plan issue link 657→ - Marker is cleared after successful submission 658→ 659→### Lifecycle 660→ 661→``` 662→Create markers → Save plan → Update roadmap → Submit → Clear markers 663→``` 664→ 665→### When to Use 666→ 667→This pattern applies when a plan is created from an objective step via `/erk:objective-plan`. The markers are set automatically during that workflow. Manual marker creation is not typically needed. 668→ 669→--- 670→ 671→## State Linking Mechanisms 672→ 673→Entities are connected through GitHub's native linking and deterministic metadata. 674→ 675→### Branch → Issue 676→ 677→Branches follow the pattern `plnd/{slug}-{timestamp}`. The association between branch and plan issue is tracked in `plan-ref.json`, which is the sole source of truth for plan-to-branch mapping. 678→ 679→**Legacy format:** Older branches may use the `P{issue}-{slug}-{timestamp}` pattern (e.g., `P123-feature-name-01-15-1430`), where the issue number is encoded in the branch name. This format is considered legacy. 680→ 681→#### Objective-Linked Branches 682→ 683→When a plan is associated with an objective (via `plan.objective_id`), the branch name encodes the objective ID: 684→ 685→**Format**: `plnd/O{objective}-{slug}-{timestamp}` 686→ 687→**Example**: `plnd/O6234-consolidated-do-01-30-1128` 688→ 689→**Legacy format**: Older objective-linked branches may use `P{plan}-O{objective}-{slug}-{timestamp}` (e.g., `P6318-O6234-consolidated-do-01-30-1128`). 690→ 691→This encoding enables: 692→ 693→- **Traceability**: Branches visually indicate their objective context 694→- **Automated tracking**: Commands can extract objective ID via `extract_objective_number(branch_name)` 695→- **Workflow routing**: Objective-aware cleanup and status updates 696→ 697→The objective ID is passed through the implementation pipeline: 698→ 699→1. `plan.objective_id` is set when plan is created from an objective step 700→2. `generate_planned_pr_branch_name(..., objective_id=plan.objective_id)` encodes it into the branch name 701→3. Downstream commands extract it via `extract_objective_number(current_branch)` 702→ 703→**Backwards Compatibility**: All extraction functions work with both current and legacy formats (`plnd/O456-...`, `P123-O456-...`, and `P123-...`). 704→ 705→### PR → Issue 706→ 707→PRs are linked to issues through: 708→ 709→- **PR body**: Contains `**Plan:** #` reference 710→- **Commit message**: The `gt finalize` command adds "Closes #N" keyword to ensure issue closure on merge 711→ 712→### Issue → Workflow Run 713→ 714→The `plan-header` metadata block contains: 715→ 716→```yaml 717→last_dispatched_run_id: "1234567890" 718→last_dispatched_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z 719→``` 720→ 721→Updated by `erk exec update-plan-header` command. 722→ 723→### Workflow Run → Issue 724→ 725→The workflow receives `issue_number` as input: 726→ 727→```yaml 728→inputs: 729→ issue_number: 730→ description: "GitHub issue number to implement" 731→ required: true 732→``` 733→ 734→Available throughout as `${{ inputs.issue_number }}`. 735→ 736→### Run Discovery 737→ 738→The `distinct_id` enables finding the specific workflow run: 739→ 740→1. **Generation**: 6-char base36 created at dispatch time 741→2. **Run name**: Set to `"{issue_number}:{distinct_id}"` 742→3. **Polling**: Match runs by `displayTitle` containing `:distinct_id` 743→ 744→--- 745→ 746→## Metadata Block Reference 747→ 748→All metadata blocks use a consistent format: 749→ 750→````html 751→ 752→
753→ {key} 754→ 755→ ```yaml {structured_data} ``` 756→
757→ 758→```` 759→ 760→### Block Types 761→ 762→| Block Key | Location | Purpose | 763→| --------------------------- | ------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | 764→| `plan-header` | Issue body | Plan metadata (created_at, dispatched_at, etc.) | 765→| `plan-body` | Issue first comment | Full plan content in collapsible details | 766→| `submission-queued` | Issue comment | Marks submission to queue | 767→| `workflow-started` | Issue comment | Links to specific workflow run | 768→| `erk-implementation-status` | Issue comment | Progress updates during implementation | 769→| `erk-worktree-creation` | Issue comment | Documents local worktree creation | 770→ 771→### `plan-header` Schema 772→ 773→```yaml 774→created_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z 775→created_by: username 776→last_dispatched_at: 2025-01-15T11:00:00Z # null if never dispatched 777→last_dispatched_run_id: "1234567890" # null if never dispatched 778→last_local_impl_at: 2025-01-15T12:00:00Z # null if never implemented locally 779→lifecycle_stage: planned # null if not yet tracked (see Lifecycle Stage Tracking) 780→``` 781→ 782→### `submission-queued` Schema 783→ 784→```yaml 785→schema: submission-queued 786→queued_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z 787→submitted_by: username 788→issue_number: 123 789→validation_results: 790→ issue_is_open: true 791→ has_erk_plan_label: true 792→expected_workflow: erk-impl 793→``` 794→ 795→### `workflow-started` Schema 796→ 797→```yaml 798→schema: workflow-started 799→status: started 800→started_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z 801→workflow_run_id: "1234567890" 802→workflow_run_url: https://github.com/owner/repo/actions/runs/1234567890 803→branch_name: 123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 804→issue_number: 123 805→``` 806→ 807→### `erk-implementation-status` Schema 808→ 809→```yaml 810→status: in_progress # pending, in_progress, complete, failed 811→completed_nodes: 3 812→total_nodes: 5 813→timestamp: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z 814→node_description: "Implementing feature X" # optional 815→``` 816→ 817→### `erk-worktree-creation` Schema 818→ 819→```yaml 820→worktree_name: 123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 821→branch_name: 123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 822→timestamp: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z 823→issue_number: 123 # optional 824→``` 825→ 826→--- 827→ 828→## Quick State Reconstruction 829→ 830→### From Issue Number 831→ 832→```bash 833→# Get issue details 834→gh issue view 123 --json title,body,comments,labels 835→ 836→# Find branches for this issue (by naming convention: 123-*) 837→git branch -r | grep "origin/123-" 838→ 839→# Find associated PR (by branch name) 840→BRANCH=$(git branch -r | grep "origin/123-" | head -1 | sed 's/origin\///') 841→gh pr list --head "$BRANCH" 842→ 843→# Find workflow runs 844→gh run list --workflow=plan-implement.yml | grep "123:" 845→``` 846→ 847→### From Branch Name 848→ 849→```bash 850→# Get branch info 851→git log origin/123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 --oneline -5 852→ 853→# Find PR 854→gh pr view 123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 855→ 856→# Check for .erk/impl-context/ 857→git ls-tree origin/123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 | grep impl-context 858→``` 859→ 860→### From PR Number 861→ 862→```bash 863→# Get PR details 864→gh pr view 456 --json title,body,headRefName 865→ 866→# Get linked issues via GitHub's native linking 867→gh pr view 456 --json closingIssuesReferences -q '.closingIssuesReferences[].number' 868→``` 869→ 870→### From Workflow Run 871→ 872→```bash 873→# Get run details 874→gh run view 1234567890 875→ 876→# Extract issue from run name (format: "123:abc123") 877→gh run view 1234567890 --json displayTitle -q '.displayTitle' | cut -d: -f1 878→``` 879→ 880→--- 881→ 882→## `.impl/plan-ref.json` Dependency Contract 883→ 884→The `.impl/plan-ref.json` file (with legacy fallback to `issue.json`) is a critical worktree setup contract. Several commands depend on it: 885→ 886→### Commands That Read `.impl/plan-ref.json` 887→ 888→| Command | Behavior if Missing | 889→| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | 890→| `erk exec get-closing-text` | Returns empty string (PR lacks "Closes #N") | 891→| `erk exec impl-signal` | Fails silently (no GitHub comment posted) | 892→| `/erk:plan-implement` | Continues without issue tracking | 893→ 894→### Silent Failure Pattern 895→ 896→The most insidious failure is with `get-closing-text`: 897→ 898→1. Worktree setup skips creating `.impl/plan-ref.json` 899→2. Implementation proceeds normally 900→3. PR is created without "Closes #N" in commit message 901→4. Issue remains open after PR merge 902→ 903→**Detection:** Check PR commit messages for "Closes #N" reference. 904→ 905→**Recovery:** Manually close the issue or amend the commit message. 906→ 907→### Ensuring Contract is Met 908→ 909→When setting up implementation environments: 910→ 911→```bash 912→# Always verify after setup 913→if [ ! -f .impl/plan-ref.json ] && [ ! -f .impl/issue.json ]; then 914→ echo "ERROR: Missing plan-ref.json - issue linking will fail" 915→ exit 1 916→fi 917→``` 918→ 919→--- 920→ 921→## Plan Metadata Field Population Lifecycle 922→ 923→Different plan fields are populated at different lifecycle stages: 924→ 925→| Field | Planning | Submitted | Implementing | Landed | 926→| ------------------------ | --------- | --------- | ------------ | ------ | 927→| `issue_number` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 928→| `title` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 929→| `created_at` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 930→| `created_by` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 931→| `lifecycle_stage` | `planned` | `planned` | `impl` | — | 932→| `branch_name` | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 933→| `pr_number` | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 934→| `last_dispatched_at` | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | 935→| `last_dispatched_run_id` | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | 936→ 937→### Why `branch_name` is null During Planning 938→ 939→During the planning stage: 940→ 941→- Plan exists only as a GitHub issue with `erk-plan` label 942→- No branch has been created yet 943→- Branch is created during `erk pr submit` (Phase 2) 944→ 945→**Implication:** Workflows that need branch information must verify the plan has been submitted (check for `branch_name` field). 946→ 947→### Graceful Failure Patterns for Metadata 948→ 949→Commands that depend on plan-header metadata should handle missing fields gracefully: 950→ 951→#### Example: `get-pr-for-plan` Dependency 952→ 953→The `get-pr-for-plan` command requires `branch_name` from plan-header: 954→ 955→```bash 956→erk exec get-pr-for-plan 957→``` 958→ 959→**Failure modes:** 960→ 961→1. **Plan not submitted** (`branch_name` is null) → Returns sentinel value `no-branch-in-plan` 962→2. **Metadata read failure** → Returns error with diagnostic message 963→ 964→**Correct handling:** 965→ 966→```bash 967→PR_NUMBER=$(erk exec get-pr-for-plan ) 968→ 969→if [ "$PR_NUMBER" = "no-branch-in-plan" ]; then 970→ echo "Plan has not been submitted yet (no branch created)" 971→ exit 1 972→fi 973→ 974→if [ "$PR_NUMBER" = "error" ]; then 975→ echo "Failed to read plan metadata" 976→ exit 1 977→fi 978→ 979→# PR_NUMBER is valid 980→gh pr view "$PR_NUMBER" 981→``` 982→ 983→#### Validation Checklist Before Plan Dispatch 984→ 985→Before dispatching a plan for implementation, validate: 986→ 987→| Check | Command | Expected | 988→| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | --------------- | 989→| Plan exists | `erk exec get-issue-body ` | `success: true` | 990→| Has erk-plan label | Check `labels` field in output | Contains label | 991→| Branch exists | `erk exec get-plan-metadata branch_name` | Non-null value | 992→| PR exists | `erk exec get-pr-for-plan ` | PR number | 993→| Not already running | Check for `workflow-started` comment on issue | No such comment | 994→| Issue is open | Check `state` field in `get-issue-body` output | `OPEN` | 995→ 996→#### When `get-pr-for-plan` Becomes Available 997→ 998→The `get-pr-for-plan` command becomes functional after: 999→ 1000→1. **Plan submission** completes (Phase 2: `erk pr submit`) 1001→2. **Branch and PR creation** finishes 1002→3. **Metadata update** writes `branch_name` to plan-header 1003→ 1004→**Timeline:** 1005→ 1006→- **Planning** (Phase 1): ❌ Not available (`no-branch-in-plan`) 1007→- **Submitted** (Phase 2): ✅ Available (returns PR number) 1008→- **Implementing** (Phase 4): ✅ Available 1009→- **Merged** (Phase 5): ✅ Available (PR still exists, just closed) 1010→ 1011→**Anti-pattern:** 1012→ 1013→```bash 1014→# DON'T: Assume get-pr-for-plan always succeeds 1015→PR_NUMBER=$(erk exec get-pr-for-plan ) 1016→gh pr view "$PR_NUMBER" # Fails if plan not submitted 1017→``` 1018→ 1019→**Correct pattern:** 1020→ 1021→```bash 1022→# DO: Check for sentinel value 1023→PR_NUMBER=$(erk exec get-pr-for-plan ) 1024→ 1025→if [ "$PR_NUMBER" = "no-branch-in-plan" ]; then 1026→ echo "Plan has not been submitted yet. Run: erk pr submit " 1027→ exit 1 1028→fi 1029→ 1030→gh pr view "$PR_NUMBER" 1031→``` 1032→ 1033→--- 1034→ 1035→## Lifecycle Stage Tracking 1036→ 1037→The `lifecycle_stage` field in the plan-header metadata block provides machine-readable tracking of a plan's current position in the lifecycle. This field is set automatically by various commands as a plan progresses. 1038→ 1039→### Stage Values 1040→ 1041→| Stage | Meaning | Color (TUI) | 1042→| ---------- | -------------------------------------------- | ----------- | 1043→| `prompted` | Plan issue created, planning not yet started | magenta | 1044→| `planning` | Plan is being written by an agent | magenta | 1045→| `planned` | Plan written, ready for implementation | dim | 1046→| `impl` | Implementation in progress or complete | yellow | 1047→ 1048→Legacy values `implementing` and `implemented` are accepted by schema validation for backwards compatibility but are never written. The display layer renders all three as `[yellow]impl[/yellow]`. See [Lifecycle Stage Consolidation](lifecycle-stage-consolidation.md) for details. 1049→ 1050→The field is nullable — plans created before this feature have `lifecycle_stage: null`, and the TUI falls back to inferring stage from PR metadata (draft state, open/merged/closed), displaying `impl` (yellow) for non-draft open PRs. 1051→ 1052→### Write Points 1053→ 1054→Each stage is set by specific commands at well-defined moments: 1055→ 1056→| Stage | Set By | When | 1057→| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | 1058→| `prompted` | `one_shot_dispatch` | One-shot plan issue created | 1059→| `planning` | `one-shot.yml` workflow | Agent begins writing plan | 1060→| `planned` | `plan_save`, `plan create`, `register_one_shot_plan`, `PlannedPRBackend.create_plan` | Plan saved to GitHub | 1061→| `impl` | `mark-impl-started`, `impl-signal` (started/submitted), `handle-no-changes`, `pr/shared.py` | Implementation begins, completes, or PR submitted | 1062→ 1063→### Explicit Updates via Exec Command 1064→ 1065→The `update-plan-header` exec command allows explicit field updates, including lifecycle stage: 1066→ 1067→```bash 1068→erk exec update-plan-header 123 lifecycle_stage=impl 1069→``` 1070→ 1071→Returns JSON on success: 1072→ 1073→```json 1074→{ "success": true, "plan_id": "123", "fields_updated": ["lifecycle_stage"] } 1075→``` 1076→ 1077→The backend validates that the plan exists, the field names are valid, and enum values (like lifecycle_stage) are one of the allowed values. 1078→ 1079→### Display Computation 1080→ 1081→`compute_lifecycle_display()` in `erk_shared.gateway.plan_data_provider.lifecycle` computes the display string for TUI tables: 1082→ 1083→1. Reads `lifecycle_stage` from plan-header fields (preferred) 1084→2. Falls back to inferring from PR metadata (`is_draft` + `pr_state`) for plans without the field 1085→3. Returns Rich color-coded markup 1086→ 1087→The fallback inference handles two additional terminal states not in the `LifecycleStageValue` type: `merged` (green) and `closed` (dim red). These are derived from PR state, not stored in plan-header. 1088→ 1089→### Stacked State 1090→ 1091→The `is_stacked` parameter is an input to both `compute_status_indicators()` and `format_lifecycle_with_status()`. It is derived from `base_ref_name` on `PullRequestInfo`: if the PR's base branch is not master/main, the PR is stacked. When `is_stacked` is `True`, a pancake emoji (🥞) is prepended to the indicator list. This is an informational indicator that does not block the rocket emoji (🚀) in the implemented stage. 1092→ 1093→See [Stacked PR Indicator](../tui/stacked-pr-indicator.md) for the full detection strategy and indicator classification. 1094→ 1095→--- 1096→ 1097→## Planned PR Lifecycle 1098→ 1099→Planned-PR-backed plans differ from issue-based plans in their storage model and branch creation timing. Instead of a separate plan issue and implementation PR, planned-PR plans evolve through lifecycle stages within a single PR. 1100→ 1101→**Key differences from issue-based plans:** 1102→ 1103→| Aspect | Issue-Based | Planned PR | 1104→| -------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------------- | 1105→| Plan storage | GitHub issue body | Draft PR body | 1106→| Branch creation | At implementation time | At plan-save time | 1107→| Plan/PR relationship | Separate entities | Same entity | 1108→| `Closes #N` footer | Links plan issue to PR | Not used (self-referential) | 1109→ 1110→**Lifecycle stages:** Plan Creation (Stage 1) → Implementation (Stage 2) → Review & Merge (Stage 3) 1111→ 1112→**Branch sync:** Because the branch is created during plan-save and the user returns to their original branch, implementation must sync with remote before starting work. 1113→ 1114→See [Planned PR Lifecycle](planned-pr-lifecycle.md) for body format details and [Planned PR Branch Sync](planned-pr-branch-sync.md) for the sync mechanism. 1115→ 1116→--- 1117→ 1118→## Related Documentation 1119→ 1120→- [Planning Workflow](workflow.md) - `.impl/` folder structure and commands 1121→- [Planned PR Lifecycle](planned-pr-lifecycle.md) - Planned PR body format and stage transitions 1122→- [Planned PR Branch Sync](planned-pr-branch-sync.md) - Branch sync during planned-PR implementation 1123→- [Exec Command Patterns](../cli/exec-command-patterns.md) - Available `erk exec` commands 1124→- [Glossary](../glossary.md) - Erk terminology definitions 1125→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

--- title: Plan Lifecycle last_audited: "2026-02-11 00:00 PT" audit_result: clean read_when: - "creating a plan" - "closing a plan" - "understanding plan states" tripwires: - action: "manually creating an erk-plan issue with gh issue create" warning: "Use `erk exec plan-save --plan-file ` instead. Manual creation requires complex metadata block format (see Metadata Block Reference section)." - action: "saving a plan linked to an objective" warning: "Always verify the link was saved correctly with `erk exec get-plan-metadata objective_issue`. Silent failures can leave plans unlinked from their objectives." - action: "implementing custom PR/plan relevance assessment logic" warning: "Reference `/local:check-relevance` verdict classification system first. Use SUPERSEDED (80%+ overlap), PARTIALLY_IMPLEMENTED (30-80% overlap), DIFFERENT_APPROACH, STILL_RELEVANT, NEEDS_REVIEW categories for consistency." - action: "after plan-implement execution completes" warning: "Always clean .erk/impl-context/ with `git rm -rf .erk/impl-context/` and commit. Transient artifacts cause CI formatter failures (Prettier)." - action: "implementing PR body generation with checkout footers" warning: "HTML `
` tags will fail `has_checkout_footer_for_pr()` validation. Use plain text backtick format: `` `gh pr checkout ` ``" - action: "calling commands that depend on `.impl/plan-ref.json` metadata" warning: "Verify metadata file exists in worktree; if missing, operations silently return empty values. read_plan_ref() tries plan-ref.json first, falls back to legacy issue.json." - action: "delegating batch file renames from a plan" warning: "Verify each file path exists before delegating. Wrong paths cause silent coverage gaps in rename operations." - action: "renaming a lifecycle stage value" warning: "Update 3 locations: LifecycleStageValue type, valid_stages set, and color conditions in compute_lifecycle_display(). Missing any location causes silent validation failures or incorrect TUI colors." score: 7 --- # Plan Lifecycle Complete documentation for the erk plan lifecycle from creation through merge. The lifecycle is currently GitHub-specific but uses provider-agnostic abstractions (PlanRef, PlanProviderType) designed for future provider generalization. ## Table of Contents - [Executive Summary](#executive-summary) - [Understanding Investigation Findings](#understanding-investigation-findings) - [Phase 1: Plan Creation](#phase-1-plan-creation) - [Phase 2: Plan Submission](#phase-2-plan-submission) - [Phase 3: Workflow Dispatch](#phase-3-workflow-dispatch) - [Phase 4: Implementation](#phase-4-implementation) - [Phase 5: PR Finalization & Merge](#phase-5-pr-finalization--merge) - [State Linking Mechanisms](#state-linking-mechanisms) - [Metadata Block Reference](#metadata-block-reference) - [Quick State Reconstruction](#quick-state-reconstruction) --- ## Executive Summary The erk plan lifecycle manages implementation plans from creation through automated execution and PR merge. ### Lifecycle Overview ``` ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ │ Create │────▶│ Submit │────▶│ Dispatch │────▶│ Implement │────▶│ Merge │ │ Plan │ │ Plan │ │ Workflow │ │ Plan │ │ PR │ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ GitHub Issue git branch GitHub Actions Code Changes Issue Closed with erk-plan creates branch finds existing committed via commit label + draft PR PR and executes and pushed message ``` ### Key File Locations at a Glance | Location | Purpose | | ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | | `~/.claude/plans/*.md` | Local plan storage (sorted by modification time) | | `.impl/plan.md` | Immutable plan in worktree (local implementation) | | `.impl/progress.md` | Mutable progress tracking | | `.impl/plan-ref.json` | Plan reference (provider-agnostic, replaces legacy issue.json) | | `.impl/run-info.json` | GitHub Actions run reference (remote only) | | `.erk/impl-context/` | Remote implementation folder (GitHub Actions) | ### Which Phase Am I In? | Observable State | Current Phase | `lifecycle_stage` | | --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ----------------- | | Issue has `erk-plan` label, no comments | Phase 1: Created | `planned` | | Issue has `submission-queued` comment | Phase 2: Submitted | `planned` | | Issue has `workflow-started` comment | Phase 3: Dispatched | `impl` | | PR is draft, workflow running | Phase 4: Implementing | `impl` | | PR is ready for review | Phase 5: Complete | `impl` | | Issue is CLOSED | Merged (PR closed the issue) | — | **Note:** The `lifecycle_stage` field in plan-header metadata provides a machine-readable equivalent of these observable states. See [Lifecycle Stage Tracking](#lifecycle-stage-tracking) for details. ### Plan Relevance Assessment When evaluating whether a plan should be implemented or closed, use the verdict classification system from `/local:check-relevance`: | Verdict | Overlap | Meaning | | --------------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------- | | SUPERSEDED | >80% | Work is already implemented in master | | PARTIALLY_IMPLEMENTED | 30-80% | Some work exists, plan may need scoping adjustment | | DIFFERENT_APPROACH | N/A | Same problem solved with different implementation | | STILL_RELEVANT | <30% | Work is not yet implemented, plan remains valid | | NEEDS_REVIEW | Unclear | Manual review required, evidence inconclusive | **Usage:** Run `/local:check-relevance ` to assess a plan's current relevance before deciding to implement or close it. ### Session Idempotency Plan save operations are idempotent within a session. The `plan-save` command: 1. Checks if a plan issue was already created for this session ID 2. If found, returns the existing issue instead of creating a duplicate 3. Uses `_get_existing_saved_issue()` helper to query GitHub This prevents duplicate issues when retry loops occur (e.g., hook blocking → retry → would-be duplicate). ### Plan Storage Lookup Priority When looking up plan files, the system checks in order: | Priority | Location | Condition | | -------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------- | | 1 | `--plan-file` argument | Always checked first | | 2 | `.erk/scratch/sessions/{id}/` | With `--session-id` | | 3 | `~/.claude/plans/` (by mtime) | Fallback | See [Plan Lookup Strategy](plan-lookup-strategy.md) for details on session-scoped lookups. --- ## Understanding Investigation Findings When a plan includes an "Investigation Findings" section, these are **mandatory corrections** that MUST be incorporated, not optional suggestions. ### What Investigation Findings Are Investigation findings appear in plans (especially erk-learn consolidation plans) after an agent: 1. **Validates original assumptions** - Checks if files exist, features are implemented, APIs work as documented 2. **Discovers reality mismatches** - Finds when original plans referenced non-existent files, outdated APIs, or completed work 3. **Documents corrections** - Records what actually exists vs what was planned **Example from a real plan:** ```markdown ## Investigation Findings ### Corrections to Original Plans - **#6134**: `prompt-executor-gateway.md` still references Haiku - confirmed needs update - **#6131**: `preprocessing.md` exists (81 lines) - needs UPDATE not CREATE - **#6130**: Pattern templates don't exist anywhere - confirmed CREATE needed ``` ### Why They Matter Without investigation findings, agents would: - Create duplicate files that already exist - Update non-existent files - Implement features that are already complete - Reference APIs that have changed Investigation findings **correct the plan** to match current reality. ### How to Use Investigation Findings When implementing a plan with investigation findings: 1. **Read them first** - Before starting implementation, understand what changed 2. **Trust them completely** - They reflect actual codebase investigation 3. **Follow their directives** - "UPDATE not CREATE" means use Edit, not Write 4. **Don't second-guess** - The investigation was thorough **Anti-pattern:** Skipping investigation findings and following original plan items that were later corrected. **Correct pattern:** Use investigation findings to understand what actions to take, then refer to implementation steps for execution order. --- ## Phase 1: Plan Creation Plans can be created through two paths: interactive (via Claude) or CLI (direct). ### Interactive Path: Plan Mode + `/erk:plan-save` The interactive path uses Claude's plan mode for guided plan creation: ```bash # 1. Enter Plan Mode (automatic for complex tasks) # 2. Create plan interactively # 3. Exit Plan Mode # 4. Save to GitHub: /erk:plan-save ``` This workflow: 1. Claude enters Plan Mode for the task 2. Plan creation with context extraction 3. Plan saved to `~/.claude/plans/*.md` on Exit Plan Mode 4. `/erk:plan-save` creates GitHub Issue with `erk-plan` label ### CLI Path: `erk pr create --file ` Direct plan creation from a file: ```bash erk pr create --file my-plan.md ``` This creates a GitHub Issue directly from the plan file. ### Plan Storage Plans are stored in GitHub Issues: - **Issue body**: Contains `plan-header` metadata block - **First comment**: Contains `plan-body` with full plan content in collapsible details **Issue body structure:** ````markdown # Plan: [Title]
plan-header ```yaml created_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z created_by: username last_dispatched_at: null last_dispatched_run_id: null last_local_impl_at: null lifecycle_stage: planned ``` ````
``` **First comment structure:** ```markdown
plan-body [Full plan content here]
``` ### The `erk-plan` Label The `erk-plan` label marks issues as implementation plans: - **Auto-created** if it doesn't exist (green, #0E8A16) - **Required** for submission and implementation - **Validated** before workflow dispatch --- ## Phase 2: Plan Submission Submission prepares the plan for remote execution via `erk pr submit `. **Key responsibility**: `erk pr submit` is the **source of truth** for branch and PR creation. The workflow dispatch (Phase 3) expects these to already exist. ### Pre-Submission Validation Before submission, the command validates: 1. **Label check**: Issue must have `erk-plan` label 2. **State check**: Issue must be OPEN (not closed) 3. **Clean working directory**: No uncommitted changes ### Branch Reuse Detection Before creating a new branch, `erk pr submit` checks for existing local branches matching the plan's branch pattern: ``` Found existing local branch(es) for this issue: • plnd/feature-01-10-0900 • plnd/feature-01-12-1430 New branch would be: plnd/feature-01-15-1600 Use existing branch 'plnd/feature-01-12-1430'? [Y/n] ``` **Note:** Legacy issue-based plans may still use the `P{issue_number}-*` pattern. Current plans use the `plnd/` prefix. **User options:** 1. **Use existing** (default): Continue with the most recent branch 2. **Delete and create new**: Remove existing branches, start fresh 3. **Abort**: Cancel submission This prevents branch proliferation when resubmitting plans. ### Branch Creation Branches are created directly via git: ```bash git branch ``` **Branch naming**: Erk computes the branch name using `sanitize_worktree_name()` with a timestamp suffix. Branch names follow the pattern `plnd/{slug}-{timestamp}` where the slug is derived from the plan title. **Example**: Plan "Add user authentication" → `plnd/add-user-authentic-11-30-1430` **Legacy format**: Older plans may use the `P{issue}-{slug}-{timestamp}` format (e.g., `P123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430`). The `P{issue}-` prefix is considered legacy; `plan-ref.json` is now the sole source of truth for plan-to-branch mapping. ### Learn Plan Base Branch Selection Learn plans (issues with `erk-learn` label) use special base branch logic: 1. **Extract parent reference**: Read `learned_from_issue` from plan-header metadata 2. **Fetch parent plan**: Get the parent implementation plan's issue 3. **Get parent branch**: Extract `branch_name` from parent's plan-header 4. **Stack on parent**: Use parent's branch as base instead of trunk This creates a branch hierarchy: ``` trunk (main) └── plnd/feature-branch-01-15-1430 (parent implementation) └── plnd/docs-for-feature-01-16-0900 (learn plan) ``` **Note:** Legacy plans may still use the `P{issue}-` prefix pattern. Current plans use `plnd/` prefix. **Fallback**: If parent lookup fails (missing parent, no branch recorded), falls back to trunk. **Implementation**: See `get_learn_plan_parent_branch()` in `src/erk/cli/commands/pr/dispatch_cmd.py`. ### `.erk/impl-context/` Folder Creation The submit command creates the `.erk/impl-context/` folder structure: ``` .erk/impl-context/ ├── plan.md # Full plan content from issue └── ref.json # Plan reference metadata (provider, plan_id, url, etc.) ``` **`ref.json` structure:** ```json { "provider": "github", "plan_id": "123", "url": "https://github.com/owner/repo/issues/123", "created_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z", "synced_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z", "labels": [], "objective_id": null } ``` ### Draft PR Creation A draft PR is created locally (for correct commit attribution): - **Title**: Issue title with "Plan: " prefix stripped - **Body**: Includes checkout instructions and metadata - **State**: Draft (marked ready after implementation) **Note**: The PR body includes `**Plan:** #` to link back to the issue. Issue closing is handled via commit message keywords ("Closes #N") when the PR is merged. ### `distinct_id` Generation A 6-character base36 identifier is generated for workflow run discovery: - Used in workflow `run-name` for matching - Enables polling to find the specific run - Format: `{issue_number}:{distinct_id}` in run display title ### Metadata Update After submission, the issue receives a `submission-queued` comment with metadata: ```yaml schema: submission-queued queued_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z submitted_by: username issue_number: 123 validation_results: issue_is_open: true has_erk_plan_label: true expected_workflow: erk-impl ``` --- ## Phase 3: Workflow Dispatch The `plan-implement.yml` workflow handles remote implementation. ### Workflow Inputs | Input | Description | | -------------- | ------------------------------------ | | `issue_number` | GitHub issue number to implement | | `submitted_by` | GitHub username of submitter | | `distinct_id` | 6-char base36 for run discovery | | `issue_title` | Issue title for workflow run display | ### Concurrency Control ```yaml concurrency: group: implement-issue-${{ github.event.inputs.issue_number }} cancel-in-progress: true ``` This ensures only one implementation runs per issue at a time. ### Workflow Phases #### Phase 1: Checkout & Setup - Checkout repository with full history - Install tools: `uv`, `erk`, `claude`, `prettier` - Configure git with submitter identity - Detect trunk branch (main or master) #### Phase 2: Find PR & Checkout Branch - Find existing PR via `gh pr list --head ` (by branch, not body search) - Checkout the implementation branch - Update `.erk/impl-context/` with fresh plan content (for reruns) #### Phase 3: Use Existing PR - Use existing PR (created by `erk pr submit`) - Post `workflow-started` comment to issue - Update issue body with `last_dispatched_run_id` #### Phase 4: Implementation - Copy `.erk/impl-context/` to `.impl/` (Claude reads `.impl/`) - Create `.impl/run-info.json` with workflow run details - Execute `/erk:plan-implement` with Claude #### Phase 5: Submission - Stage implementation changes (NOT `.erk/impl-context/` deletion) - Run `/erk:git-pr-push` to create proper commit message - Clean up `.erk/impl-context/` in separate commit - Mark PR ready for review - Update PR body with implementation summary - Trigger CI via empty commit --- ## Phase 4: Implementation Implementation executes the plan, whether locally or via GitHub Actions. ### `.erk/impl-context/` vs `.impl/` | Folder | Purpose | Git Status | | -------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | | `.erk/impl-context/` | Remote implementation (GitHub Actions) | Committed, then deleted | | `.impl/` | Local implementation + Claude's working copy | In `.gitignore`, never committed | In GitHub Actions, `.erk/impl-context/` is copied to `.impl/` before Claude runs. ### `.impl/run-info.json` Created in GitHub Actions to track the workflow run: ```json { "run_id": "1234567890", "run_url": "https://github.com/owner/repo/actions/runs/1234567890" } ``` ### `/erk:plan-implement` Command The implementation command: 1. Validates `.impl/` exists with `plan.md` and `progress.md` 2. Creates TodoWrite entries for tracking 3. Posts start comment to GitHub issue (if linked) 4. Executes each phase sequentially 5. Updates `progress.md` as steps complete 6. Runs CI validation 7. Cleans up artifacts ### Progress Tracking Progress is tracked in `.impl/progress.md`: ```markdown --- completed_nodes: 3 total_nodes: 5 steps: - text: "1. First step" completed: true - text: "2. Second step" completed: true - text: "3. Third step" completed: true - text: "4. Fourth step" completed: false - text: "5. Fifth step" completed: false --- # Progress Tracking - [x] 1. First step - [x] 2. Second step - [x] 3. Third step - [ ] 4. Fourth step - [ ] 5. Fifth step ``` Progress tracking is done via the TodoWrite tool in the Claude Code session. ### Detecting Queued vs Implemented Plans A PR associated with a plan may exist but not contain the actual implementation: - **Queued Plan**: PR contains only `.erk/impl-context/` folder with plan files - **Implemented Plan**: PR contains actual source code changes To verify implementation status: 1. Check if PR diff includes changes outside `.erk/impl-context/` 2. Use `gh pr diff ` and look for actual implementation files 3. Don't rely solely on PR state (OPEN/MERGED) - a PR can be open with only plan files This pattern was discovered when verifying prerequisite PR #5577: the PR existed and was open, but only contained `.erk/impl-context/` plan files, not the actual PlanSynthesizer agent. ### No-Changes Error Scenario When implementation produces no code changes (duplicate plan, work already merged), the workflow handles this gracefully: 1. **Detects no changes**: Branch has no commits beyond base 2. **Creates diagnostic PR**: Updates PR body with diagnostic information 3. **Applies `no-changes` label**: Marks PR for user review 4. **Posts issue comment**: Links issue to diagnostic PR 5. **Exits gracefully**: Returns exit code 0 (success) **Exit code semantics:** - Exit 0 = Success (PR updated and ready for review) - Exit 1 = Error (GitHub API failure) The workflow treats no-changes as successful completion, not an error. Users review the diagnostic PR to determine if work is already done. See [No Code Changes Handling](no-changes-handling.md) for details. --- ## Phase 5: PR Finalization & Merge The final phase prepares the PR for review and merge. ### `/erk:git-pr-push` Submission The pure git submission flow: 1. Analyze staged changes 2. Generate AI commit message 3. Commit with proper attribution 4. Push to remote 5. Update PR body with summary ### `.erk/impl-context/` Cleanup In GitHub Actions, `.erk/impl-context/` is removed in a separate commit after CI passes: ```bash git rm -rf .erk/impl-context/ git commit -m "Remove .erk/impl-context/ folder after implementation" git push ``` This keeps the implementation commit clean. #### Timing and Distinction The cleanup happens in a specific sequence: 1. **Implementation commit** - Contains the actual code changes 2. **CI validation** - Tests, formatting, type checking must pass 3. **Remove `.erk/impl-context/`** - Cleanup commit (this step) 4. **Push changes** - Both commits pushed to PR **Key distinction:** - **`.erk/impl-context/`**: CI automatically removes this after validation passes. This is the ephemeral working copy used during remote implementation. It's safe to delete because it's already been copied to `.impl/` for Claude's use. - **`.impl/`**: Requires user review and manual deletion. This is the original plan that should be preserved until the user confirms completion. Never auto-delete this folder - it serves as documentation of what was planned vs. what was implemented. **Clear sequence**: CI passes → remove `.erk/impl-context/` → commit → push This pattern ensures that transient artifacts (`.erk/impl-context/`) are cleaned up automatically while permanent artifacts (`.impl/`) remain for user review. ### PR Ready for Review ```bash gh pr ready "$BRANCH_NAME" ``` Marks the draft PR as ready for review. ### PR Body Update The PR body is updated with: 1. Implementation summary (from commit message) 2. Standardized footer from `get-pr-body-footer` 3. Checkout instructions ### CI Trigger An empty commit triggers push-event workflows: ```bash git commit --allow-empty -m "Trigger CI workflows" git push ``` This is needed because workflow dispatch doesn't trigger PR workflows. ### Auto-Close on Merge GitHub automatically closes the linked issue when the PR is merged if the commit message contains "Closes #N" or similar keywords. The `gt finalize` command (used during PR finalization) adds the closing keyword to the commit message, ensuring the issue is closed when the PR merges. --- ## Objective Roadmap Integration When implementing a plan that corresponds to an objective roadmap step, the workflow includes automatic roadmap updates via markers. ### Marker-Based State Management 1. **Create markers during planning:** - `objective-context` marker: stores objective issue number (read by plan-save to link the plan to its parent objective) - `roadmap-step` marker: stores step ID (e.g., "1C.2") for automatic roadmap updates 2. **Automatic roadmap update on plan save:** - `/erk:plan-save` checks for `roadmap-step` marker - If present, runs `erk exec update-objective-node` to update the objective's roadmap table with the plan issue link - Marker is cleared after successful submission ### Lifecycle ``` Create markers → Save plan → Update roadmap → Submit → Clear markers ``` ### When to Use This pattern applies when a plan is created from an objective step via `/erk:objective-plan`. The markers are set automatically during that workflow. Manual marker creation is not typically needed. --- ## State Linking Mechanisms Entities are connected through GitHub's native linking and deterministic metadata. ### Branch → Issue Branches follow the pattern `plnd/{slug}-{timestamp}`. The association between branch and plan issue is tracked in `plan-ref.json`, which is the sole source of truth for plan-to-branch mapping. **Legacy format:** Older branches may use the `P{issue}-{slug}-{timestamp}` pattern (e.g., `P123-feature-name-01-15-1430`), where the issue number is encoded in the branch name. This format is considered legacy. #### Objective-Linked Branches When a plan is associated with an objective (via `plan.objective_id`), the branch name encodes the objective ID: **Format**: `plnd/O{objective}-{slug}-{timestamp}` **Example**: `plnd/O6234-consolidated-do-01-30-1128` **Legacy format**: Older objective-linked branches may use `P{plan}-O{objective}-{slug}-{timestamp}` (e.g., `P6318-O6234-consolidated-do-01-30-1128`). This encoding enables: - **Traceability**: Branches visually indicate their objective context - **Automated tracking**: Commands can extract objective ID via `extract_objective_number(branch_name)` - **Workflow routing**: Objective-aware cleanup and status updates The objective ID is passed through the implementation pipeline: 1. `plan.objective_id` is set when plan is created from an objective step 2. `generate_planned_pr_branch_name(..., objective_id=plan.objective_id)` encodes it into the branch name 3. Downstream commands extract it via `extract_objective_number(current_branch)` **Backwards Compatibility**: All extraction functions work with both current and legacy formats (`plnd/O456-...`, `P123-O456-...`, and `P123-...`). ### PR → Issue PRs are linked to issues through: - **PR body**: Contains `**Plan:** #` reference - **Commit message**: The `gt finalize` command adds "Closes #N" keyword to ensure issue closure on merge ### Issue → Workflow Run The `plan-header` metadata block contains: ```yaml last_dispatched_run_id: "1234567890" last_dispatched_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z ``` Updated by `erk exec update-plan-header` command. ### Workflow Run → Issue The workflow receives `issue_number` as input: ```yaml inputs: issue_number: description: "GitHub issue number to implement" required: true ``` Available throughout as `${{ inputs.issue_number }}`. ### Run Discovery The `distinct_id` enables finding the specific workflow run: 1. **Generation**: 6-char base36 created at dispatch time 2. **Run name**: Set to `"{issue_number}:{distinct_id}"` 3. **Polling**: Match runs by `displayTitle` containing `:distinct_id` --- ## Metadata Block Reference All metadata blocks use a consistent format: ````html
{key} ```yaml {structured_data} ```
```` ### Block Types | Block Key | Location | Purpose | | --------------------------- | ------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | | `plan-header` | Issue body | Plan metadata (created_at, dispatched_at, etc.) | | `plan-body` | Issue first comment | Full plan content in collapsible details | | `submission-queued` | Issue comment | Marks submission to queue | | `workflow-started` | Issue comment | Links to specific workflow run | | `erk-implementation-status` | Issue comment | Progress updates during implementation | | `erk-worktree-creation` | Issue comment | Documents local worktree creation | ### `plan-header` Schema ```yaml created_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z created_by: username last_dispatched_at: 2025-01-15T11:00:00Z # null if never dispatched last_dispatched_run_id: "1234567890" # null if never dispatched last_local_impl_at: 2025-01-15T12:00:00Z # null if never implemented locally lifecycle_stage: planned # null if not yet tracked (see Lifecycle Stage Tracking) ``` ### `submission-queued` Schema ```yaml schema: submission-queued queued_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z submitted_by: username issue_number: 123 validation_results: issue_is_open: true has_erk_plan_label: true expected_workflow: erk-impl ``` ### `workflow-started` Schema ```yaml schema: workflow-started status: started started_at: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z workflow_run_id: "1234567890" workflow_run_url: https://github.com/owner/repo/actions/runs/1234567890 branch_name: 123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 issue_number: 123 ``` ### `erk-implementation-status` Schema ```yaml status: in_progress # pending, in_progress, complete, failed completed_nodes: 3 total_nodes: 5 timestamp: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z node_description: "Implementing feature X" # optional ``` ### `erk-worktree-creation` Schema ```yaml worktree_name: 123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 branch_name: 123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 timestamp: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z issue_number: 123 # optional ``` --- ## Quick State Reconstruction ### From Issue Number ```bash # Get issue details gh issue view 123 --json title,body,comments,labels # Find branches for this issue (by naming convention: 123-*) git branch -r | grep "origin/123-" # Find associated PR (by branch name) BRANCH=$(git branch -r | grep "origin/123-" | head -1 | sed 's/origin\///') gh pr list --head "$BRANCH" # Find workflow runs gh run list --workflow=plan-implement.yml | grep "123:" ``` ### From Branch Name ```bash # Get branch info git log origin/123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 --oneline -5 # Find PR gh pr view 123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 # Check for .erk/impl-context/ git ls-tree origin/123-add-user-authentic-11-30-1430 | grep impl-context ``` ### From PR Number ```bash # Get PR details gh pr view 456 --json title,body,headRefName # Get linked issues via GitHub's native linking gh pr view 456 --json closingIssuesReferences -q '.closingIssuesReferences[].number' ``` ### From Workflow Run ```bash # Get run details gh run view 1234567890 # Extract issue from run name (format: "123:abc123") gh run view 1234567890 --json displayTitle -q '.displayTitle' | cut -d: -f1 ``` --- ## `.impl/plan-ref.json` Dependency Contract The `.impl/plan-ref.json` file (with legacy fallback to `issue.json`) is a critical worktree setup contract. Several commands depend on it: ### Commands That Read `.impl/plan-ref.json` | Command | Behavior if Missing | | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | | `erk exec get-closing-text` | Returns empty string (PR lacks "Closes #N") | | `erk exec impl-signal` | Fails silently (no GitHub comment posted) | | `/erk:plan-implement` | Continues without issue tracking | ### Silent Failure Pattern The most insidious failure is with `get-closing-text`: 1. Worktree setup skips creating `.impl/plan-ref.json` 2. Implementation proceeds normally 3. PR is created without "Closes #N" in commit message 4. Issue remains open after PR merge **Detection:** Check PR commit messages for "Closes #N" reference. **Recovery:** Manually close the issue or amend the commit message. ### Ensuring Contract is Met When setting up implementation environments: ```bash # Always verify after setup if [ ! -f .impl/plan-ref.json ] && [ ! -f .impl/issue.json ]; then echo "ERROR: Missing plan-ref.json - issue linking will fail" exit 1 fi ``` --- ## Plan Metadata Field Population Lifecycle Different plan fields are populated at different lifecycle stages: | Field | Planning | Submitted | Implementing | Landed | | ------------------------ | --------- | --------- | ------------ | ------ | | `issue_number` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | `title` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | `created_at` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | `created_by` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | `lifecycle_stage` | `planned` | `planned` | `impl` | — | | `branch_name` | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | `pr_number` | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | `last_dispatched_at` | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | | `last_dispatched_run_id` | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ### Why `branch_name` is null During Planning During the planning stage: - Plan exists only as a GitHub issue with `erk-plan` label - No branch has been created yet - Branch is created during `erk pr submit` (Phase 2) **Implication:** Workflows that need branch information must verify the plan has been submitted (check for `branch_name` field). ### Graceful Failure Patterns for Metadata Commands that depend on plan-header metadata should handle missing fields gracefully: #### Example: `get-pr-for-plan` Dependency The `get-pr-for-plan` command requires `branch_name` from plan-header: ```bash erk exec get-pr-for-plan ``` **Failure modes:** 1. **Plan not submitted** (`branch_name` is null) → Returns sentinel value `no-branch-in-plan` 2. **Metadata read failure** → Returns error with diagnostic message **Correct handling:** ```bash PR_NUMBER=$(erk exec get-pr-for-plan ) if [ "$PR_NUMBER" = "no-branch-in-plan" ]; then echo "Plan has not been submitted yet (no branch created)" exit 1 fi if [ "$PR_NUMBER" = "error" ]; then echo "Failed to read plan metadata" exit 1 fi # PR_NUMBER is valid gh pr view "$PR_NUMBER" ``` #### Validation Checklist Before Plan Dispatch Before dispatching a plan for implementation, validate: | Check | Command | Expected | | ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | --------------- | | Plan exists | `erk exec get-issue-body ` | `success: true` | | Has erk-plan label | Check `labels` field in output | Contains label | | Branch exists | `erk exec get-plan-metadata branch_name` | Non-null value | | PR exists | `erk exec get-pr-for-plan ` | PR number | | Not already running | Check for `workflow-started` comment on issue | No such comment | | Issue is open | Check `state` field in `get-issue-body` output | `OPEN` | #### When `get-pr-for-plan` Becomes Available The `get-pr-for-plan` command becomes functional after: 1. **Plan submission** completes (Phase 2: `erk pr submit`) 2. **Branch and PR creation** finishes 3. **Metadata update** writes `branch_name` to plan-header **Timeline:** - **Planning** (Phase 1): ❌ Not available (`no-branch-in-plan`) - **Submitted** (Phase 2): ✅ Available (returns PR number) - **Implementing** (Phase 4): ✅ Available - **Merged** (Phase 5): ✅ Available (PR still exists, just closed) **Anti-pattern:** ```bash # DON'T: Assume get-pr-for-plan always succeeds PR_NUMBER=$(erk exec get-pr-for-plan ) gh pr view "$PR_NUMBER" # Fails if plan not submitted ``` **Correct pattern:** ```bash # DO: Check for sentinel value PR_NUMBER=$(erk exec get-pr-for-plan ) if [ "$PR_NUMBER" = "no-branch-in-plan" ]; then echo "Plan has not been submitted yet. Run: erk pr submit " exit 1 fi gh pr view "$PR_NUMBER" ``` --- ## Lifecycle Stage Tracking The `lifecycle_stage` field in the plan-header metadata block provides machine-readable tracking of a plan's current position in the lifecycle. This field is set automatically by various commands as a plan progresses. ### Stage Values | Stage | Meaning | Color (TUI) | | ---------- | -------------------------------------------- | ----------- | | `prompted` | Plan issue created, planning not yet started | magenta | | `planning` | Plan is being written by an agent | magenta | | `planned` | Plan written, ready for implementation | dim | | `impl` | Implementation in progress or complete | yellow | Legacy values `implementing` and `implemented` are accepted by schema validation for backwards compatibility but are never written. The display layer renders all three as `[yellow]impl[/yellow]`. See [Lifecycle Stage Consolidation](lifecycle-stage-consolidation.md) for details. The field is nullable — plans created before this feature have `lifecycle_stage: null`, and the TUI falls back to inferring stage from PR metadata (draft state, open/merged/closed), displaying `impl` (yellow) for non-draft open PRs. ### Write Points Each stage is set by specific commands at well-defined moments: | Stage | Set By | When | | ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | | `prompted` | `one_shot_dispatch` | One-shot plan issue created | | `planning` | `one-shot.yml` workflow | Agent begins writing plan | | `planned` | `plan_save`, `plan create`, `register_one_shot_plan`, `PlannedPRBackend.create_plan` | Plan saved to GitHub | | `impl` | `mark-impl-started`, `impl-signal` (started/submitted), `handle-no-changes`, `pr/shared.py` | Implementation begins, completes, or PR submitted | ### Explicit Updates via Exec Command The `update-plan-header` exec command allows explicit field updates, including lifecycle stage: ```bash erk exec update-plan-header 123 lifecycle_stage=impl ``` Returns JSON on success: ```json { "success": true, "plan_id": "123", "fields_updated": ["lifecycle_stage"] } ``` The backend validates that the plan exists, the field names are valid, and enum values (like lifecycle_stage) are one of the allowed values. ### Display Computation `compute_lifecycle_display()` in `erk_shared.gateway.plan_data_provider.lifecycle` computes the display string for TUI tables: 1. Reads `lifecycle_stage` from plan-header fields (preferred) 2. Falls back to inferring from PR metadata (`is_draft` + `pr_state`) for plans without the field 3. Returns Rich color-coded markup The fallback inference handles two additional terminal states not in the `LifecycleStageValue` type: `merged` (green) and `closed` (dim red). These are derived from PR state, not stored in plan-header. ### Stacked State The `is_stacked` parameter is an input to both `compute_status_indicators()` and `format_lifecycle_with_status()`. It is derived from `base_ref_name` on `PullRequestInfo`: if the PR's base branch is not master/main, the PR is stacked. When `is_stacked` is `True`, a pancake emoji (🥞) is prepended to the indicator list. This is an informational indicator that does not block the rocket emoji (🚀) in the implemented stage. See [Stacked PR Indicator](../tui/stacked-pr-indicator.md) for the full detection strategy and indicator classification. --- ## Planned PR Lifecycle Planned-PR-backed plans differ from issue-based plans in their storage model and branch creation timing. Instead of a separate plan issue and implementation PR, planned-PR plans evolve through lifecycle stages within a single PR. **Key differences from issue-based plans:** | Aspect | Issue-Based | Planned PR | | -------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------------- | | Plan storage | GitHub issue body | Draft PR body | | Branch creation | At implementation time | At plan-save time | | Plan/PR relationship | Separate entities | Same entity | | `Closes #N` footer | Links plan issue to PR | Not used (self-referential) | **Lifecycle stages:** Plan Creation (Stage 1) → Implementation (Stage 2) → Review & Merge (Stage 3) **Branch sync:** Because the branch is created during plan-save and the user returns to their original branch, implementation must sync with remote before starting work. See [Planned PR Lifecycle](planned-pr-lifecycle.md) for body format details and [Planned PR Branch Sync](planned-pr-branch-sync.md) for the sync mechanism. --- ## Related Documentation - [Planning Workflow](workflow.md) - `.impl/` folder structure and commands - [Planned PR Lifecycle](planned-pr-lifecycle.md) - Planned PR body format and stage transitions - [Planned PR Branch Sync](planned-pr-branch-sync.md) - Branch sync during planned-PR implementation - [Exec Command Patterns](../cli/exec-command-patterns.md) - Available `erk exec` commands - [Glossary](../glossary.md) - Erk terminology definitions
